rulings.Case Title: NATASJA NIELSEN VS. SECUREFRAME, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANTS SHRAV MEHTA,, SECUREFRAME, INC.'S MOTION FOR AN ORDER TO SEAL CERTAIN JUDICIAL RECORDS FILED IN THIS CASE IN THE INTEREST OF JUSTICE (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Thursday, May 29, 2025, line 2, DEFENDANTS SHRAV MEHTA,, SECUREFRAME, INC.'S MOTION FOR AN ORDER TO SEAL CERTAIN JUDICIAL RECORDS FILED IN THIS CASE IN THE INTEREST OF JUSTICE (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2) The court is not keen to revisit the same issues now. In any case, Defendant's present interest in confidentiality does not predominate. The court is confident that a substantial portion of the allegedly confidential information is available through other public documents, including SEC filings. Defendant has not established a substantial probability exists that its cognizable privacy interest will be prejudiced if the records are not sealed. Defendant's claims of harm from disclosure are quite general and unpersuasive. In fact, these records have been public for three years and Defendant has not identified a single actual issue arising from public access. The proposed sealing is not narrowly tailored. The court has reviewed the complaint and the Schaffer Declaration and Defendant has no colorable privacy claim as to the vast majority of information contained in them. It is of little significance that the motion is unopposed. The court has reviewed the documents Defendant seeks to seal and finds they do not meet the criteria set out in Rule 2.550. Defendant must prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the time set for hearing. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 302 Zoom ID 160 409 7690; Passcode 516287.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. (end of tenative ruling part 2 of 2) = (302/JMQ))

rulings.Case Title: NATASJA NIELSEN VS. SECUREFRAME, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: / Unopposed Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order To Seal Certain Judicial Records Filed In This Case In The Interest Of Justice; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support; Declaration Of Shrav Mehta; And Declaration Of Omair M. Farooqui
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Thursday, May 29, 2025, line 2, DEFENDANTS SHRAV MEHTA,, SECUREFRAME, INC.'S Motion For An Order To Seal Certain Judicial Records Filed In This Case In The Interest Of Justice (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Defendant Secrueframe, Inc.'s unopposed Motion For An Order To Seal Certain Judicial Records Filed In This Case In The Interest Of Justice is DENIED. A court may order that a record be filed under seal only if it expressly finds facts that establish: (1) an overriding interest exists that overcomes the right of public access to the record; (2) the overriding interest supports sealing the records; (3) a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; (4) the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and, (5) no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. (Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 2.550(d)(1)-(5); NBC Subsidiary (KNBC-TV), Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (Locke) (1999) 20 Cal.4th 1178, 1216-1218 ("NBC Subsidiary").) The party seeking the sealing of a record bears the burden of presenting information sufficient to identify the specific information claimed to be entitled to such treatment and the nature of the harm threatened by disclosure. (H.B. Fuller Co. v. Doe (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 879, 894.) The moving party must set forth specific facts in support of a motion to seal; statements that are too conclusory, abstract, or generic are not sufficient to satisfy the burden. (Id. at pp. 895-896.) Here, Plaintiff asks the court to seal: (1) Plaintiff's Verified Complaint, filed August 29, 2022; and (2) Declaration of J. Andrew Schaffer in Support of Defendant's Unopposed Ex Parte Application to replace Currently filed Complaint with New Version of Complaint, filed Nov. 1, 2022. Defendant here did not satisfy its burden of presenting specific facts demonstrating that specific information sealed and the nature of the harm threatened by disclosure. The public right of access to adjudicative facts is quite high and difficult to overcome. Afterall, the public has a keen interest in knowing whether the court is properly applying the law; to know this, it must have access to the adjudicative facts and the relevant legal discussion. The court finds the information sought to be sealed amounts to adjudicative facts, i.e., the basic allegations upon which relief was sought and the basic facts supporting an extraordinary order swapping a complaint for another nunc pro tunc based on Defendant's privacy and confidentiality concerns. First, the court already addressed Defendant's concerns about private and confidential information in the initial complaint and authorized removal of the initial complaint and replacement with a sanitized version. (See Order, filed Nov. 2, 2022.) If the parties accomplished the swap, that should resolve the matter (and the parties certainly did not suggest they may have to come back three years later to address the same concerns). If the parties did not accomplish the swap, that's on them. Similarly, as to the Shaffer Declaration, given the purpose of the declaration was to address and resolve Defendant's privacy and confidentiality concerns, it is reasonable to believe the parties would have ensured the declaration did not run afoul of those concerns; if it did, the issue should have been addressed then. (end of tenative ruling part 1, see part 2) = (302/JMQ))

rulings.Case Title: TALIA KENNEDY VS. META PLATFORMS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Confidential Document In Support Of Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Thursday, May 29, 2025, line 3, 1 - DEFENDANTS ETHAN LUBKA, IAN LAIKS, META PLATFORMS, INC. (F/K/A FACEBOOK, INC.) Motion To Seal Confidential Document In Support Of Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Defendants Ethan Lubka, Ian Laiks and Meta Platforms, Inc.'s Motion To Seal Confidential Document In Support Of Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication is GRANTED. A court may order that a record be filed under seal only if it expressly finds facts that establish: (1) an overriding interest exists that overcomes the right of public access to the record; (2) the overriding interest supports sealing the records; (3) a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; (4) the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and, (5) no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. (Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 2.550(d)(1)-(5); NBC Subsidiary (KNBC-TV), Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (Locke) (1999) 20 Cal.4th 1178, 1216-1218 ("NBC Subsidiary").) The party seeking the sealing of a record bears the burden of presenting information sufficient to identify the specific information claimed to be entitled to such treatment and the nature of the harm threatened by disclosure. (H.B. Fuller Co. v. Doe (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 879, 894.) The moving party must set forth specific facts in support of a motion to seal; statements that are too conclusory, abstract, or generic are not sufficient to satisfy the burden. (Id. at pp. 895-896.) Here, Defendants ask the court to seal a single document identified as BATES TK000173 - TK000175. Defendants satisfied their burden. The public right of access to adjudicative facts is quite high and difficult to overcome. Afterall, the public has a keen interest in knowing whether the court is properly applying the law; to know this, it must have access to the adjudicative facts and the relevant legal discussion. The court finds the information sought to be sealed amounts to adjudicative facts on the MSJ/MSA. Of course, if the information did not amount to adjudicative facts relevant to the MSJ/MSA, it would not be necessary for any party to submit it in connection with the MSJ/MSA. The public has a right of access. The interest in confidentiality, however, predominates. The court finds an overriding interest exists that overcomes the right of public access to this specific record. The overriding privacy interest in the single document supports sealing the record. And, the court finds, absent sealing, it is substantially probable that the identified privacy interest would be prejudiced. The proposed sealing is narrowly tailored, and no less restrictive means exist to preserve the overriding privacy interest. (See Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 2.550(e).) The court notes it is of little significance that the parties stipulated to a protective order or agreed to keep certain information confidential. Such agreements are not governed by Rule 2.550 and they do not carry much weight in the relevant analysis, which focuses on the public's right to access. (end of Tenative ruling part 1,see part 2)

rulings.Case Title: ETHAN SUTIN VS. X CORP., A NEVADA CORPORATION
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss, Or In The Alternative Stay, Action Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure 410.30
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Thursday, May 29, 2025, line 4, DEFENDANT X CORPORATION'S Motion To Dismiss, Or In The Alternative Stay, Action Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure 410.30 (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Defendant X Corporation's Motion to Dismiss, or in the Alternative, Stay Action is DENIED. Under CCP 410.30(a), "[w]hen a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just." Here, defendant failed to file this motion within a reasonable time and at this point, enforcing the clause would be unjust. (Trident Labs, Inc. v. Merrill Lynch Commercial Finance Corp. (2011) 200 Cal.App.4th 147 [denying non conveniens motion because 19-month delay was unreasonable]; Global Financial Distributors, Inc. v. Superior Court (2019) 35 Cal.App.5th 179, 193 ["a party must bring such a motion to enforce a forum selection clause within a reasonable time."].) Plaintiff first attempted to resolve this dispute through arbitration, but defendant failed to proffer the arbitration fees. (Proctor Decl., pars. 3-4.) Thereafter, on September 1, 2023, plaintiff filed this action. Defendant answered on October 26, 2023, and the parties subsequently engaged in party and third-party discovery and other litigation. (Proctor Decl., par. 7.) Defendant filed this motion on March 25, 2025. Defendant's delay in bringing this motion was unreasonable. Defendant's contention that settlement negotiations and plaintiff's refusal to sit for deposition caused the delay ring hollow. Defendant argues that it needed to determine whether plaintiff freely entered into the Restriction Agreement. (Terry Decl., par. 4.) But defendant could have relied on other discovery tools to determine plaintiff's assent to the Restriction Agreement and brought this motion earlier. Notably, the original complaint recognizes the legitimacy of that agreement and relies on it to recover. (See, e.g., Complaint, pars. 3, 33, 38, 40.) (end of tentative ruling part 1, see part 2) = (302/JMQ)

rulings.Case Title: HENRY YOUNESIAN VS. PAUL DICKERSON ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Thursday, May 29, 2025, line 5, 2 - DEFENDANT PAUL DICKERSON MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST Amended COMPLAINT Defendant Paul Dickerson's Motion to Strike is DENIED. Defendant moves under Code of Civil Procedure section 435. "Any party, within the time allowed to respond to a pleading may serve and file a notice of motion to strike the whole or any part thereof" (Code of Civil Procedure section 435.) "The court may, upon a motion made pursuant to Section 435, or at any time in its discretion, and upon terms it deems proper: [para] (a) Strike out any irrelevant, false, or improper matter inserted in any pleading. (b) Strike out all or any part of any pleading not drawn or filed in conformity with the laws of this state, a court rule, or an order of the court." (Code of Civil Procedure section 436.) Here, Defendant's motion fails. Defendant's premise is Plaintiff's Labor Code claim fails; if so, the requests for an attachment and attorney's fees under the Labor Code fail. The court, however, has found Plaintiff has alleged a claim under Labor Code section 3700 et seq. The requests for an attachment and fees are, thus, viable. (See Labor Code sections 3707 and 3709.) Labor Code section 3709 expressly provides for an award of attorney's fees to a prevailing employee in a civil action against an uninsured employer. The court is not persuaded by Defendant's argument (unaccompanied by any citations to authority) regarding "compensation." For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 302 Zoom ID 160 409 7690; Passcode 516287.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (302/JMQ))

rulings.Case Title: ROULAC GLOBAL VS. FUNDERA, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Thursday, May 29, 2025, line 7, DEFENDANT FUNDERA, INC., NERDWALLET, INC. DEMURRER to COMPLAINT Defendants Fundera Inc. and NerdWallet's demurrer is SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND. *THE COURT'S FULL AND COMPLETE TENTATIVE RULING HAS BEEN EMAILED TO COUNSEL* For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 302 Zoom ID 160 409 7690; Passcode 516287.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (302/JMQ))
